explanation_md: `count` equals 3, so the vector is initialised with three elements all set to 10. Then `qty[banana]` (index 1) is overwritten with 5. The three values printed are therefore 10, 5, 10.

prompt_md:
Given `enum Color { red, green, blue, count };`, which expression correctly creates a `std::vector<int>` with exactly one slot per colour?

a) `std::vector<int> v(Color::count);`
b) `std::vector<int> v(count);`
c) `std::vector<int> v(count - 1);`
d) `std::vector<int> v(count + 1);`

options.a: `std::vector<int> v(Color::count);` — scoped-enum syntax for an unscoped enum
options.b: `std::vector<int> v(count);` — sentinel equals the number of real slots
options.c: `std::vector<int> v(count - 1);` — one less than the sentinel

explanation_md: For an unscoped enum, `count` equals 3 (one past the last real enumerator), which is exactly the number of slots needed. Option (a) uses scope-resolution on an unscoped enum — that compiles but is not idiomatic. Options (c) and (d) produce the wrong size.

options.a: The vector should be constructed with `count + 1` elements
options.b: The loop condition `i <= count` accesses index `count`, which is out of bounds
options.c: Unscoped enumerators cannot be used as vector subscripts
options.d: The initialiser list form should be used instead of the size constructor

explanation_md: Valid indices are 0 through `count - 1` (i.e., 0, 1, 2). The condition `i <= count` lets `i` reach the value `count` (3), which is one past the last valid index — undefined behaviour. The correct condition is `i < count`.

prompt_md:
Why do scoped enumerators (`enum class`) require an explicit cast when used as vector subscripts, while unscoped enumerators do not?

a) `enum class` enumerators have a different underlying type than `int`
b) `enum class` enumerators do not implicitly convert to integer types
c) `std::vector` subscript operator only accepts `enum class` values
d) Scoped enumerators start at 1 by default instead of 0

options.a: `enum class` enumerators have a different underlying type than `int`
options.b: `enum class` enumerators do not implicitly convert to integer types
options.c: `std::vector` subscript operator only accepts `enum class` values
options.d: Scoped enumerators start at 1 by default instead of 0

explanation_md: Scoped enumerations deliberately suppress implicit conversions to integers — that is their main safety benefit. The underlying type is still `int` by default, and enumerators still start at 0, so options (a) and (d) are wrong. Option (c) is the opposite of reality: the subscript operator accepts integers, not scoped enumerators directly.
